name: Sarah Chen
notes: Co-founder. Sets company strategy and owns investor relationships. Drives the Series C narrative — needs £12M+ ARR story by H2 2026. Increasingly focused on AI transformation story as a competitive differentiator. Made the controversial decision to hire Oliver Mwangi as VP Engineering externally, over Marcus's strong preference to promote Yuki.

name: Marcus Webb
notes: Co-founder. Owns the technical roadmap and engineering org. Championed the tech debt freeze in Q1 2026. Skeptical of AI hype but open to specific, high-ROI use cases. Strongly opposed the decision to hire Oliver Mwangi externally — considered promoting Yuki a matter of trust and institutional respect. Now manages both Oliver and Yuki in the engineering org.

name: Priya Sharma
notes: Manages a team of 14 CSMs. Deeply frustrated by the broken public roadmap commitment from Product. Sits on the Customer Advisory Board with top-tier accounts. Was vindicated by the onboarding NPS data after dec-003 — the hiring of Wei Zhang (actor-019) as Head of Customer Onboarding is a partial acknowledgment that dec-003 was a mistake, though this was never said publicly.

name: Tom Adeyemi
notes: Owns product strategy and the roadmap. Was the primary skeptic of moving to Linear from Jira. Now a convert. Currently navigating tension between tech debt freeze and customer commitments. Manages Amara Diallo (Senior PM — Platform) who carries scar tissue from the 2024 CS triage AI pilot failure.

name: Yuki Tanaka
notes: Runs day-to-day engineering execution. Now reports to Oliver Mwangi (actor-013, VP Engineering) after Oliver's hire in 2023 narrowed Yuki's scope from org management to technical execution. This is a source of ongoing friction — Marcus strongly opposed the hire and considers it a trust violation. Yuki has remained professional but her engagement has visibly changed. Manages 22 engineers across 3 squads. Champions the engineering runbook library initiative.

name: Nadia Kovalchuk
notes: Owns month-end close and financial reporting to the board. Kwame Asante (actor-020) is her Financial Controller and does most of the day-to-day reconciliation work — the 38-hour month-end close burden falls primarily on Kwame. Nadia is the primary internal champion for AI in finance workflows and is building the Series C financial model.

name: James Okafor
notes: Technical lead for the Finance Workflow Engine — Veridian's highest-revenue module. Point person for the Hartfield SSO integration. Raised concerns internally about the commitment timeline before it was made. Now reports to Yuki under Oliver's expanded org structure.

name: Fatima Al-Rashid
notes: Joined 18 months ago. Hired externally — controversial decision that bypassed two internal candidates. Now owns cross-functional ops, including the commitment tracking problem Veridian has quietly ignored. Direct reports include: Priya Sharma (CS), Tom Adeyemi (Product), Nadia Kovalchuk (Finance), Anya Volkov (People), Imogen Walsh (Legal).

name: Chloe Beaumont
notes: Directly manages the Hartfield Group and Meridian Capital accounts. Delivered the consolidated bug report format that Engineering requested. Informally owns institutional knowledge about customer commitments — keeps a personal Notion doc tracking them.

name: Ravi Patel
notes: Owns new business and expansion revenue. Was the dissenting voice when Veridian chose Salesforce over HubSpot. Now manages Carlos Mendez (Solutions Engineering) as part of the go-to-market org. Immediately flagged attribution complexity when Sophie Laurent joined with HubSpot — a concern that has proven correct 18 months later.

name: Dev Anand
notes: Owns the Customer Data Platform. Built the Looker dashboards used by CS and Sales. Sits at the intersection of Engineering and the AI readiness conversation — understands data quality issues better than anyone. Technically aware of the Intercom-Salesforce sync issues that Ben Osei owns.

name: Anya Volkov
notes: Owns HR, recruiting, and people ops. Key stakeholder in the COO hiring decision. Runs the quarterly planning process that surfaces cross-functional commitments — but currently has no system to track them after the meeting. Owns the New Employee Onboarding workflow (wf-018), which hasn't been updated since 2024.

name: Oliver Mwangi
notes: Hired externally from Google UK in early 2023 to manage 3 engineering squads and own the technical org as Marcus moved to CTO-level. This hire was controversial — Marcus strongly favored promoting Yuki Tanaka and considered the external hire a trust violation. Yuki was retained as Head of Engineering but her scope was narrowed to technical execution vs. org management. Oliver reports to Marcus; Yuki and the squad leads now report to Oliver. Has a promise outstanding to Marcus: all 3 engineering squads documented in runbook coverage by end of Q1 2026 — only Squad 1 was complete by the deadline (com-013, broken).

name: Sophie Laurent
notes: Owns demand gen and brand. Joined with a strong HubSpot preference — the split-stack decision (dec-011) was made partly to accommodate her. Frustrated that there is no campaign-to-closed-deal attribution in Salesforce due to the HubSpot-Salesforce sync never working cleanly. Has committed to Sarah (com-014) that attribution will be working by Q2 2026, but the integration work depends on Ben Osei who has not scheduled it.

name: Ben Osei
notes: Owns the Intercom-Salesforce integration that has the known data quality issues flagged in wf-002. Also the engineer who would need to do the HubSpot-Salesforce attribution sync work that Sophie Laurent is committed to delivering by Q2 2026. That work is not currently in any sprint. Ben's team sits under Oliver Mwangi's org.

name: Imogen Walsh
notes: Reviews all enterprise contracts and security questionnaires. The primary bottleneck on legal review turnaround — the range is 2 days to 3 weeks depending on workload. This variability is the #1 reason deals slip past their expected close date according to Ravi Patel. No answer library exists for common security questionnaire questions — every one is answered from scratch.

name: Carlos Mendez
notes: Runs technical demos and security reviews for enterprise deals. Has the most up-to-date knowledge of customer integration requirements — he's the person who actually writes responses to security questionnaires, despite having no answer library to draw from. Close collaborator with Imogen Walsh on legal-security reviews. Reports to Ravi Patel.

name: Amara Diallo
notes: Owns the Finance Workflow Engine roadmap. Was the primary PM on the failed 2024 CS triage AI pilot (dec-002) and carries some residual wariness about AI initiatives that aren't properly scoped. This context is important when proposing new AI use cases to the Product team — Amara will ask hard questions about change management, not just technology.

name: Wei Zhang
notes: Dedicated onboarding lead hired specifically because onboarding time had grown from 3 to 6 weeks after onboarding was reabsorbed by CS in 2023 (dec-003). Wei's creation as a role partially reverses dec-003 — a dedicated specialist is back — though this was never framed publicly as admitting a mistake. By Q4 2024, onboarding time was back to 4 weeks. Reports to Priya Sharma.

name: Kwame Asante
notes: Handles day-to-day finance operations. In practice, Kwame is the person who does the 38-hour month-end close each month — the manual Xero→Salesforce reconciliation, the payroll reconciliation, the expense categorization. He is the primary beneficiary of any AI automation of wf-001. Reports to Nadia Kovalchuk.
